25 Dec: Most things closed; some hotel restaurants and a few central restaurants open. Public transport on holiday Sunday timetable.

26 Dec (Boxing Day / 2. juledag): Things start reopening — many restaurants open, museums often open, cinemas and bowling alleys see family traffic. Boxing Day is when Oslo "wakes up" after Christmas Eve.

🎆 New Year's Eve (Nyttårsaften) — 31 December 2026

Important and counter-intuitive: Oslo no longer organises a municipal fireworks display, and private fireworks are prohibited in central Oslo (including Bjørvika). The fireworks you'll see are sporadic private displays across the fjord.

Best viewing spots:

  • Operahuset roof — THE main viewpoint, literally next door to Operagata 27, free, 24/7. Wear ice grippers (the slope can be packed; crampons sold at any pharmacy/Apotek for ~150 NOK)
  • Aker Brygge / Tjuvholmen waterfront — lively crowds, harbour views
  • Ekebergparken — elevated, panoramic
  • Holmenkollen — high vantage but cold and remote

Restaurant set menus: Nearly all sold out by November. Hotel restaurants and Maaemo, Statholdergaarden, Theatercafeen all do New Year service. A laser light show typically projects from City Hall (Rådhuset).

1 January: Public holiday — most things closed; public transport on Sunday/holiday timetable. Oslo Konserthus traditionally hosts a New Year concert with the Filharmonien.

🎵 Major Festivals — 2026 At a Glance

Festival 2026 Dates What
Holmenkollen Ski Festival 13–15 March (Nordic) + 19–22 March (biathlon) World Cup ski jumping, cross-country, biathlon
Inferno Metal Festival 2–5 April Extreme metal at Rockefeller
Musikkfest Oslo Sat 6 June Free city-wide music marathon
Piknik i Parken 11–13 June Indie pop at Sofienbergparken
Oslo Pride 17–27 June (Parade Sat 27 June) Norway's biggest LGBTQ+ celebration
Sankthansaften Tue 23 June Midsummer bonfires
Oslo Jazz Festival 9–15 August 70+ concerts across 10–15 venues
Øyafestivalen 12–15 August Norway's biggest music festival, Tøyenparken
Mela Festival 14–16 August Free multicultural fest at Rådhusplassen
by:Larm 10–12 September Music industry showcase (Nordic SXSW)
Kulturnatt Fri 11 September Free late-night cultural marathon
Oslo Marathon Sat 12 September Marathon + half + 10K
Ultima Festival 10–19 September Contemporary/experimental music
Oslo World 27 Oct – 1 Nov World music festival
Vinterland opens ~7 November Christmas market at Spikersuppa
Norsk Folkemuseum Christmas Fair 6–7 + 13–14 December Bygdøy heritage market
The Nutcracker Late Nov – early Jan Den Norske Opera & Ballett (next door)

🌌 Northern Lights from Oslo? — Honest Answer

Oslo (59.9°N) is too far south for reliable aurora. You'll occasionally see it during major geomagnetic storms but cannot plan a trip around it. The 2024–2026 solar cycle peak does keep activity strong through 2026.

For real aurora: fly to Tromsø (1h45 flight, Aurora rating 9/10), Bodø, Lofoten, Alta, or Kirkenes. Late January through March in Tromsø balances aurora-darkness with longer daytime for skiing/dog sledding.

💡 Seasonal Practicalities

🎫 Oslo Pass season strategy:

  • Summer (Jun–Aug): worth it if you'll do 3+ paid attractions per day — fully covers ferries, museums, Bygdøy ferry
  • Winter (Dec–Feb): less useful; many outdoor attractions are free or seasonal-closed

🛍️ Stock the apartment in advance for these dates (everything closes):

  • Wednesday before Easter (1 April 2026)
  • 16 May (the day before Constitution Day)
  • 23 December
  • 30 December (in case you'll cook on NYE/NYD)

🍽️ Reservation lead times for festival-busy nights:

  • 17 May lunch/dinner: book 2+ weeks ahead
  • Sankthansaften dinner (23 June): book 1 week ahead
  • Christmas Eve hotel dinners: book by early November
  • New Year's Eve restaurant set menus: book by early November
  • Øya Festival weekend (12–15 August): all Bjørvika restaurants pack — book 1 week ahead

🌡️ When to book Bjørvika for the best of each season:

  • Cherry blossoms: late April – first week of May
  • Light nights peak: mid-June to early July
  • Festival peak: August (Øya, Jazz, Mela)
  • Foliage: late September – mid October
  • Christmas markets: all of December
  • Skiing + sauna combo: late January – February

👕 What to pack by season:

  • June–August: light layers, swimsuit, a fleece for boats and evening winds, compact umbrella
  • September–October: waterproof shell, walking shoes that handle wet leaves
  • November–February: insulated coat, ice grippers/crampons (essential — buy in any Apotek for ~150 NOK), wool base layers, beanie
  • March–May: layered shoulder-season kit, both sun and rain prep

💧 Tap water: Among the best on Earth — bring refillable bottles, never buy bottled.

Vi sees i Oslo — see you in Oslo. Anything you can't find here, ask me directly.

topics.title: Check-Out
topics.html:
Check-out time is at 11:00 AM on

Need a later check-out? Let me know in advance and I'll do my best. If incoming guests are arriving that day, flexibility may be limited.

✅ Before You Leave

  • Close all windows and the balcony door
  • Take all personal belongings — check nightstands, bathroom cabinets, and wardrobes
  • Leave any waste in the bins under the kitchen sink
  • Return outdoor blankets/pillows to the basket by the window (if used on the balcony)
  • Remember to lock the apartment when you leave
  • Return your key and fob to the Joker store (same place you collected them) Joker Barcode,
    Dronning Eufemias gate 6B — 📍 Map

You do not need to strip beds, start the washing machine, or clean. The team handles everything.

🧳 Luggage After Check-Out

If your flight is later in the day, I may be able to store your luggage briefly — just ask. Alternatively, Oslo Central Station has luggage lockers (80–130 NOK/day, 8-minute walk).

Lockers are on the lower level near tracks 11–13. Pay by contactless card. The largest locker fits two large suitcases plus a backpack. 

✈️ Getting to the Airport

Walk to Oslo Central Station (8 minutes). The Flytoget (19 min, every 10 min) is the fastest way to Gardermoen. Buy tickets at the station or via the Flytoget app.

Tip: Allow 2 hours before international flights, 90 minutes for domestic.

Schengen flights — security only takes 10–15 min off-peak. Outside Schengen (UK, US, etc.) plan for 90 min before boarding because of passport control. Tax-free refund desks are after security on the international side.

topics.title: House Rules & Emergency Info
topics.html:
These are in place to make sure every guest — and every neighbour — has a great experience. Most are common sense, but worth noting.

The Essentials

  • Maximum 4 guests. Only registered guests may stay overnight.
  • Quiet hours: 11:00 PM – 8:00 AM. Sound travels easily in modern buildings — please keep noise low in hallways, on the balcony, and when coming home late.
  • No parties or gatherings that create excessive noise.
  • No smoking — anywhere in the apartment, the balcony, or building common areas. This includes vaping.
  • No pets — due to guest allergies.
  • Shoes off inside — this is Norwegian custom and protects the oak parquet floors. A shoe rack is in the entrance.

Taking Care of the Space

  • Close the balcony door when not in use — coastal winds can be strong
  • Close all windows before leaving the apartment — rain can enter quickly
  • Ventilation hood on before cooking — prevents the building fire alarm from triggering
  • Please treat the appliances with care — if something isn't working or you're unsure, check this guide or message me. I'd rather help than have something break.

♻️ Waste & Recycling

Norway takes recycling seriously — and once you know the system, it's easy. Under the kitchen sink is a 4-compartment pull-out bin:

  • Green bag: Food waste (organic — compostable items, coffee grounds)
  • Purple bag: Clean plastic packaging (rinse before disposal)
  • General section: Everything else
  • Separate bins next to the pull-out: glass, paper/cardboard, and deposit bottles

Deposit bottles (pant): Most cans and plastic bottles are worth 1–3 NOK each. Return them at any grocery store's bottle machine for a receipt you can spend in-store. It's a satisfying local habit.

The building waste room is at street level next to the entrance — for overflow or larger items.

topics.title: Safety & Emergency Info
topics.html:
🚨 Emergency Numbers

  • Police: 112
  • Ambulance: 113
  • Fire: 110

English-speaking operators are always available. Call 112 from any phone if unsure which service you need.

Non-emergency police: 02800 — for theft reports, lost passports, etc.

🏥 Medical Care

Non-emergency medical advice: Call 116 117 — available 24/7. They'll assess your situation and direct you to the right care.

Oslo Legevakt (24h emergency clinic):
Storgata 40, about 15 minutes by taxi. For urgent but non-life-threatening issues.
📍 Map

Nearest pharmacy:
Apotek 1 at Oslo Central Station (Mon–Fri 7:00–21:00, Sat 9:00–18:00)
📍 Map

24-hour pharmacy: Vitusapotek Jernbanetorget — at the central station, open all night, Sundays included.
Dental emergency (24h): Tannlegevakten Oslo, call 22 67 30 00.

Tip: EU/EEA citizens should bring their European Health Insurance Card (EHIC) for reduced-cost treatment. All visitors should have travel insurance.

🔥 Fire Safety

The apartment has smoke detectors in every room and a fire extinguisher in the hallway near the entrance.

If the building fire alarm sounds:

  1. Stay calm — false alarms from cooking happen occasionally in apartment buildings
  2. Check for smoke or fire. If there's no visible danger, the alarm usually stops within 1–2 minutes
  3. If you see smoke or fire: leave via the stairwell (do not use the elevator), close the apartment door behind you, and call 110
  4. Assembly point: the courtyard in front of the building

Prevention: Always use the ventilation hood when cooking — the building's sensors are very sensitive.

🔒 Building Security

The building entrance requires your fob — keep it with you when you leave. If you get locked out, call me. I live in the same building and can let you in quickly.

💧 Tap Water

Oslo's tap water is among the cleanest in the world — safe, fresh, and delicious straight from the tap. No need for bottled water.

topics.title: Book Again
topics.html:
I'd love to host you again, . The easiest way to rebook is through the platform you originally used — search "Top Floor by Oslo Opera" or tap "Book again" from your trip history.

⭐ Returning Guest Advantage

As a returning guest, you have access to a preferred direct booking rate.

If you book directly, you’ll receive 5% lower pricing than the standard platform rate (which includes platform service fees).

To request your stay:

  • Email: reneotz@gmail.com
  • Include your preferred dates
  • I’ll confirm availability and send a tailored booking offer

Why this works well:

  • Slightly better pricing
  • Direct communication and flexibility
  • Same apartment, same standard — just a smoother process

🌅 Best Times to Visit Oslo

  • Summer (Jun–Aug): Long days with light until midnight, outdoor dining, island hopping in the fjord. Peak season — book early.
  • Winter (Nov–Feb): Cosy city vibes, Christmas markets, northern lights day trips. Lower rates available.
  • Shoulder (Apr–May, Sep–Oct): Fewer crowds, pleasant weather, autumn colours along the Akerselva river. Great value.

Availability changes quickly around weekends, holidays, and Oslo's peak travel periods — checking early is always worthwhile.

If you enjoyed your stay, sharing the listing with friends or family planning a trip to Oslo would mean a lot. Word of mouth from happy guests is the best compliment.
